patterns.name: REST Resource-Oriented API
patterns.description: Design APIs around resources (nouns) rather than actions (verbs), using HTTP methods to express operations. Each resource has a stable URI and supports standard CRUD semantics through GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE. This is the foundational pattern for RESTful service design.
principles: Resources are identified by URIs, not actions
HTTP methods convey operation semantics
Representations are decoupled from internal storage
Statelessness: each request contains all context needed
Uniform interface reduces coupling between client and server
Resources may contain links to related resources
when_to_use: Building public-facing APIs consumed by third parties
CRUD-dominant workloads with well-defined domain entities
Systems where cacheability of GET responses is valuable
Microservices exposing domain aggregates to other services
Mobile and web clients needing a predictable contract
avoid_when: Operations are inherently action-oriented (e.g., batch computation triggers)
Real-time bidirectional communication is required
Client needs to fetch deeply nested related data in a single call
Extremely low-latency binary protocols are needed
signals: Domain model has clearly identifiable nouns (users, orders, products)
Most operations map cleanly to CRUD verbs
Clients are diverse (browser, mobile, third-party)
HTTP caching headers would meaningfully reduce load
API documentation references resource lifecycles
Existing OpenAPI or Swagger specs describe the surface
key_concepts: Resource URI design (/users/{id}/orders)
Content negotiation via Accept headers
Idempotency of PUT and DELETE
Status code semantics (201 Created, 204 No Content, 404 Not Found)
ETag and conditional requests for concurrency control
HATEOAS links for discoverability

patterns.name: Richardson REST Maturity Model
patterns.description: A four-level model (0-3) that classifies API maturity from plain RPC-over-HTTP to full hypermedia-driven REST. Level 0 uses HTTP as a tunnel, Level 1 introduces resources, Level 2 adds HTTP verbs, and Level 3 adds hypermedia controls. Most production APIs target Level 2.
principles: Each maturity level builds on the previous one
Level 2 (HTTP verbs + resources) is the pragmatic sweet spot
Level 3 (HATEOAS) maximises evolvability but increases complexity
Maturity level choice should match team capability and client needs
Higher maturity does not always mean better fitness for purpose
when_to_use: Evaluating an existing API's design quality
Setting architectural guidelines for a new API platform
Deciding whether to invest in hypermedia controls
Training teams on REST design principles
Auditing API consistency across an organisation
avoid_when: The API is internal-only and RPC semantics are simpler
Team lacks experience to maintain hypermedia contracts
Performance constraints make Level 3 overhead unjustifiable
signals: API review checklist references maturity levels
Design documents mention Richardson model explicitly
Existing APIs mix verbs and nouns inconsistently
API gateway enforces verb-based routing rules
Style guide mandates specific HTTP method usage
key_concepts: Level 0: single URI, single HTTP method (POST tunnel)
Level 1: multiple URIs, one verb (resource identification)
Level 2: multiple URIs, correct HTTP verbs (verb semantics)
Level 3: hypermedia controls embedded in responses
Content-type negotiation at higher levels
Link relations for state transitions

patterns.name: HATEOAS (Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State)
patterns.description: Responses include hypermedia links that tell clients what actions are available from the current state, eliminating the need for out-of-band knowledge of the API surface. Clients navigate the API by following links rather than constructing URIs. This is Richardson Level 3 REST.
principles: Clients discover available actions from response links, not documentation
Server controls state transitions through link presence or absence
URI structure can change without breaking compliant clients
Link relations use standard or custom relation types
Entry point URI is the only URI a client needs to know
when_to_use: APIs that must evolve without breaking existing clients
Workflow-driven domains where available actions depend on state
Long-lived public APIs with diverse unknown consumers
APIs where server-driven navigation improves security
Domain models with complex state machines
avoid_when: Clients are tightly coupled internal services with shared contracts
Performance-sensitive paths where link overhead is unacceptable
Team lacks tooling to generate and consume hypermedia formats
API surface is simple CRUD with no workflow transitions
signals: Responses contain _links or links objects with rel and href
Clients follow link relations rather than building URIs
Content types include application/hal+json or similar
State-dependent actions appear/disappear in responses
API documentation describes link relations, not URI templates
key_concepts: HAL (Hypertext Application Language) format
JSON:API link objects
Link relation types (self, next, prev, related)
Affordances: embedding available methods in links
Siren entity/action model
Spring HATEOAS and similar server-side libraries

patterns.name: REST Pagination Patterns
patterns.description: Strategies for breaking large result sets into pages: offset-based, cursor-based, and keyset pagination. Each approach trades off simplicity, consistency, and performance differently. Cursor-based pagination is preferred for large or frequently-changing datasets.
principles: Never return unbounded result sets from list endpoints
Include total count only when the query cost is acceptable
Cursor-based pagination avoids offset drift on inserts/deletes
Page size should have a sensible default and a maximum cap
Pagination metadata belongs in response body or Link headers
when_to_use: Any list endpoint returning more than ~50 items
Feed-style data where new items are continuously inserted
Search result pages with forward/backward navigation
APIs consumed by infinite-scroll UIs
Data exports requiring stable iteration over large datasets
avoid_when: Result sets are always small and bounded (e.g., enum lookups)
Clients always need the complete set for local computation
Data is append-only and a simple timestamp filter suffices
signals: List endpoints accept limit and offset or cursor parameters
Responses include next_cursor or next_page_token fields
Link headers contain rel=next and rel=prev
API documentation warns about maximum page sizes
Database queries use LIMIT/OFFSET or WHERE id > cursor
UI implements infinite scroll or load-more buttons
key_concepts: Offset pagination: page number + page size
Cursor pagination: opaque token encoding position
Keyset pagination: WHERE clause on indexed column
Total count: expensive on large tables, consider omitting
Stable sort order required for consistent pagination
RFC 5988 Link header for page navigation

patterns.name: REST Filtering and Query Parameters
patterns.description: Conventions for filtering, sorting, and field selection on REST list endpoints using query parameters. Well-designed filtering reduces over-fetching and avoids the need for custom endpoints per use case. Common approaches include LHS/RHS bracket notation, dedicated filter query languages, and sparse fieldsets.
principles: Filters are query parameters, not path segments
Support compound filters without custom DSLs where possible
Field selection (sparse fieldsets) reduces payload size
Sorting parameters should support multi-field and direction
Filter parameters should map to indexed database columns
when_to_use: List endpoints with heterogeneous consumer query needs
Dashboards requiring server-side filtering and sorting
APIs where bandwidth matters (mobile clients)
Replacing multiple bespoke query endpoints with one generic one
Admin panels with dynamic table filtering
avoid_when: Queries are complex enough to warrant GraphQL
Filtering logic requires joins across multiple aggregates
Full-text search is the primary use case (use a search endpoint instead)
signals: Query strings contain filter[status]=active or status=active
Endpoints accept sort=created_at,-name parameters
Fields parameter like fields=id,name,email in requests
API documentation lists filterable and sortable fields
Backend translates query params to WHERE clauses
Response metadata includes applied filters
key_concepts: LHS bracket notation: filter[field]=value
RHS colon notation: field=gte:100
Sparse fieldsets: fields[type]=field1,field2
Multi-sort: sort=field1,-field2 (minus for descending)
Filter operators: eq, ne, gt, gte, lt, lte, in, like
JSON:API filter specification

patterns.name: URL-Based API Versioning
patterns.description: Embedding the API version in the URL path (e.g., /v1/users, /v2/users). This is the most visible and straightforward versioning strategy, making version differences explicit in routing. Trade-off is URI pollution and potential resource identity confusion across versions.
principles: Version number appears as the first path segment after the base URL
Major versions only; minor/patch changes are backward-compatible
Old versions must have a documented sunset timeline
Routing infrastructure directs traffic by version prefix
Resource identity should remain stable across versions where possible
when_to_use: Public APIs where version visibility aids developer experience
APIs with infrequent breaking changes (yearly or less)
Organisations with simple routing infrastructure
APIs where multiple versions must coexist for extended periods
Brownfield systems migrating from legacy to modern contracts
avoid_when: Breaking changes are frequent and fine-grained
Versioning individual resources independently is needed
API gateway supports header-based routing natively
Hypermedia-driven clients that follow links rather than construct URIs
signals: URLs contain /v1/, /v2/ path segments
API gateway routing rules match version prefixes
Deployment manifests reference version-specific service instances
Client SDKs expose version as a constructor parameter
Changelog documents version-to-version migration steps
key_concepts: Major version in path: /v1/resource
Semantic versioning for API contracts
Version sunset policy and deprecation headers
Parallel deployment of multiple versions
API gateway version routing
Version-specific documentation generation

patterns.name: Header-Based API Versioning
patterns.description: Using custom headers (e.g., X-API-Version) or the Accept header with vendor media types (e.g., application/vnd.company.v2+json) to specify API version. Keeps URIs clean and allows version negotiation at the content type level. Requires more sophisticated routing infrastructure.
principles: Version is a content negotiation concern, not an addressing concern
Accept header with vendor media types is the most RESTful approach
Custom version headers are simpler but less standards-compliant
Default version should be specified when no version header is sent
API gateway or middleware must parse headers for routing
when_to_use: APIs striving for RESTful purity with clean URIs
Organisations with API gateways that support header-based routing
Versioning at the representation level rather than the resource level
Internal APIs where clients can be updated to send headers
APIs with fine-grained versioning needs per resource
avoid_when: Clients cannot easily set custom headers (e.g., simple browser links)
Developer experience priorities favour visible version in URL
Infrastructure lacks header-based routing capability
API is public-facing with many unsophisticated consumers
signals: Requests include Accept: application/vnd.company.v2+json
Custom X-API-Version header in request/response
API gateway routing rules inspect Accept or custom headers
Content-Type response includes vendor media type
API documentation specifies supported media type versions
Middleware extracts version from headers before handler dispatch
key_concepts: Vendor media types: application/vnd.company.resource.v2+json
Accept header content negotiation
Custom X-API-Version header convention
Default version fallback policy
Header-based API gateway routing
Vary header for cache correctness with versioned responses

patterns.name: REST Bulk Operations
patterns.description: Patterns for creating, updating, or deleting multiple resources in a single HTTP request to reduce round-trips and improve throughput. Approaches include batch endpoints, JSON arrays in request bodies, and multipart requests. Must handle partial failures explicitly.
principles: Batch endpoint accepts an array of operations or resources
Response reports per-item success/failure status
Partial success must be clearly communicated (e.g., 207 Multi-Status)
Batch size limits prevent memory exhaustion and timeout issues
Idempotency keys enable safe retry of failed batch items
when_to_use: Importing or syncing data from external systems
Clients that need to create/update many records at once
Network-constrained environments where round-trip reduction matters
Admin operations that apply changes to multiple entities
ETL pipelines writing transformed data via API
avoid_when: Individual operations have complex validation requiring separate handling
Transactional atomicity across all items is required (use a saga instead)
Batch sizes are unpredictable and could cause timeouts
signals: Endpoint path includes /batch or /bulk suffix
Request body is a JSON array of resource objects
Response includes per-item status codes and error details
API documentation specifies maximum batch size
Idempotency-Key header required for batch requests
207 Multi-Status response code in API specification
key_concepts: Batch endpoint: POST /resources/batch
Per-item result reporting with individual status codes
207 Multi-Status HTTP response
Idempotency keys for retry safety
Maximum batch size limits and chunking
Asynchronous batch processing with status polling

patterns.name: REST Partial Updates (PATCH)
patterns.description: Using HTTP PATCH with structured patch documents to update only specific fields of a resource, avoiding the need to send the full representation. JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396) and JSON Patch (RFC 6902) are the two dominant formats. PATCH reduces bandwidth and avoids accidental field overwrites.
principles: PATCH updates only the fields specified in the request body
JSON Merge Patch is simpler but cannot set fields to null unambiguously
JSON Patch provides operation-level precision (add, remove, replace, move)
Content-Type header must specify the patch format used
Concurrent PATCH operations need optimistic locking via ETag/If-Match
when_to_use: Updating a few fields on a large resource without sending the full body
Collaborative editing where multiple clients modify different fields
Mobile clients on constrained networks minimising payload size
Resources with many optional fields where PUT would require all fields
Form-based UIs that submit only changed fields
avoid_when: The resource is small enough that PUT is equally efficient
All updates are full replacements by design
Clients cannot reliably produce patch documents
signals: API accepts PATCH method on resource URIs
Content-Type is application/merge-patch+json or application/json-patch+json
Request body contains only changed fields
ETag and If-Match headers used for concurrent modification control
API documentation distinguishes PUT (full replace) from PATCH (partial)
Validation runs only on submitted fields, not the entire resource
key_concepts: JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396): partial JSON object merge
JSON Patch (RFC 6902): array of operations (op, path, value)
Content-Type negotiation for patch format
Optimistic concurrency with ETag/If-Match
Idempotency considerations for PATCH
Validation scoping to patched fields only

patterns.name: Schema-First GraphQL Design
patterns.description: Define the GraphQL schema (SDL) before implementing resolvers, treating the schema as the contract between frontend and backend. The schema serves as documentation, type system, and API specification simultaneously. This approach promotes API-first design and clear team boundaries.
principles: Schema definition drives resolver implementation, not the reverse
Schema is the single source of truth for the API contract
Types model the domain, not the database schema
Nullability decisions are intentional and documented
Deprecation directives maintain backward compatibility
Input types are separate from output types
when_to_use: Frontend and backend teams work in parallel on different schedules
API serves multiple clients with varying data requirements
Domain model is complex with many relationships
Strong typing and introspection are valued for developer tooling
Reducing over-fetching is a priority for mobile clients
avoid_when: API surface is simple CRUD with few relationships
Team lacks GraphQL schema design experience
File uploads or binary streaming are primary use cases
Real-time requirements exceed subscription capabilities
signals: Project contains .graphql or .gql schema files
Schema-first codegen tools (graphql-codegen, Apollo codegen) in build pipeline
Type definitions precede resolver implementations
Schema review is part of the pull request process
Mock servers generated from schema for frontend development
Schema registry or schema stitching infrastructure exists
key_concepts: Schema Definition Language (SDL)
Type system: scalar, object, enum, union, interface, input
Resolver chain: root -> type -> field resolution
Schema directives (@deprecated, @auth, custom)
Schema-first codegen for type-safe resolvers
Schema linting and breaking change detection

patterns.name: GraphQL Federation
patterns.description: Compose a single GraphQL supergraph from multiple independently deployed subgraphs, each owning a portion of the schema. A gateway/router merges schemas and routes queries to the appropriate subgraphs. Enables team autonomy while presenting a unified API to consumers.
principles: Each subgraph owns its types and fields exclusively
Entity references (@key) enable cross-subgraph type extension
The gateway performs query planning and execution across subgraphs
Subgraphs are independently deployable and testable
Schema composition must detect and prevent conflicts at build time
Performance depends on efficient query plan optimisation
when_to_use: Multiple teams own different domains within a single API surface
Monolithic GraphQL server is becoming a bottleneck for deployment
Domains have clear ownership boundaries (users, products, orders)
Organisation is adopting microservices with GraphQL
Independent scaling of different domain subgraphs is needed
avoid_when: Single team owns the entire API
Schema is small enough that a monolithic server is simpler
Subgraph boundaries are unclear or frequently shifting
Latency overhead of gateway query planning is unacceptable
signals: Schema contains @key, @external, @provides, @requires directives
Apollo Router, Apollo Gateway, or similar federation runtime in infrastructure
Subgraph services register with a schema registry
CI/CD pipeline includes schema composition checks
Supergraph manifest or rover commands in build scripts
Multiple GraphQL services deployed independently
key_concepts: Subgraph: independently deployed GraphQL service
Supergraph: composed schema from all subgraphs
Entity and @key directive for cross-subgraph references
Query planning: gateway decomposes queries across subgraphs
Schema composition and conflict detection
Federated tracing for cross-subgraph observability

patterns.name: GraphQL Subscriptions
patterns.description: Server-push mechanism in GraphQL for real-time data delivery over persistent connections, typically WebSockets. Clients subscribe to events matching specific criteria and receive updates as they occur. Useful for live dashboards, chat, and collaborative features.
principles: Subscriptions use the same type system as queries and mutations
Transport is typically WebSocket (graphql-ws or subscriptions-transport-ws protocol)
Server-side pub/sub backs subscription resolution
Subscription lifecycle must handle connection drops and reconnection
Filtering subscriptions server-side reduces unnecessary data transfer
when_to_use: Live dashboards showing real-time metrics or status updates
Chat or messaging features requiring push notifications
Collaborative editing where changes must propagate to all viewers
Order tracking or delivery status updates
Notification feeds that update without polling
avoid_when: Data changes infrequently and polling is sufficient
Clients are behind proxies that do not support WebSockets
Scale requires millions of concurrent persistent connections
SSE would suffice and WebSocket complexity is not justified
signals: Schema defines Subscription root type with event fields
WebSocket endpoint configured alongside HTTP endpoint
PubSub system (Redis, Kafka) backs subscription events
Client code uses useSubscription hook or subscribe() method
Connection protocol is graphql-ws or subscriptions-transport-ws
Infrastructure supports sticky sessions or WebSocket-aware load balancing
key_concepts: Subscription root type in SDL
graphql-ws protocol (successor to subscriptions-transport-ws)
AsyncIterator-based resolver pattern
PubSub engine for event distribution
Subscription filtering arguments
Connection lifecycle: init, ack, data, complete, error

patterns.name: GraphQL DataLoader Pattern
patterns.description: Batching and caching mechanism that solves the N+1 query problem in GraphQL resolvers. DataLoader collects individual load requests within a single execution tick and issues one batched query. Per-request caching ensures the same entity is fetched only once per query execution.
principles: Batch individual loads into a single query per execution tick
Cache results per request to eliminate duplicate fetches
DataLoader instances are created per request, not globally
Batch function must return results in the same order as keys
Loader key design must match the underlying data access pattern
when_to_use: GraphQL resolvers fetch related entities that cause N+1 queries
List fields resolve child entities individually in a loop
Multiple resolvers in a single query need the same entity
Database query count must be minimised for performance
API calls to downstream services should be batched
avoid_when: Resolvers already perform batch fetches natively
Query depth is shallow with no nested entity resolution
Data source does not support batch lookups
signals: Resolver functions call loader.load(id) instead of direct DB queries
DataLoader instances created in request context/middleware
Batch functions accept arrays of keys and return arrays of results
Query execution logs show reduced database round-trips
Performance profiling identifies N+1 patterns in resolver chains
Context object passed to resolvers contains loader instances
key_concepts: Batching: coalesce loads within a single tick
Per-request caching: avoid duplicate fetches in one query
Batch function contract: keys in, ordered results out
Loader-per-resource pattern (userLoader, orderLoader)
Priming: manually populating cache from known data
Error handling: return Error objects in result array positions

patterns.name: GraphQL Persisted Queries
patterns.description: Pre-register GraphQL queries on the server and reference them by hash or ID at runtime, eliminating the need to send full query strings over the network. Reduces bandwidth, improves security by restricting executable queries, and enables query-level caching and optimisation.
principles: Query text is replaced by a compact identifier (hash or ID)
Server maintains a registry of allowed queries
Automatic persisted queries (APQ) register on first use via hash negotiation
Persisted queries enable CDN caching of GET-based GraphQL requests
Query allowlisting prevents arbitrary query execution in production
when_to_use: Reducing payload size for mobile clients on constrained networks
Preventing query injection or denial-of-service via complex queries
Enabling HTTP GET semantics for GraphQL query caching at CDN edge
Production environments where only approved queries should execute
Build pipelines that extract and register queries at deploy time
avoid_when: API is in rapid prototyping phase with constantly changing queries
Introspection and ad-hoc queries are required for developer tooling
Clients need to construct dynamic queries at runtime
signals: Client sends query hash (SHA-256) instead of query text
Server returns PersistedQueryNotFound error triggering registration
Build pipeline includes query extraction and manifest generation
CDN configuration caches GraphQL GET requests by query hash
Query allowlist enforced in production but not in development
Apollo or Relay persisted query plugin configured
key_concepts: Automatic Persisted Queries (APQ): hash-based negotiation
Static persisted queries: build-time extraction and registration
Query manifest: mapping of hash to query text
CDN caching with GET-based persisted queries
Query allowlisting for security hardening
SHA-256 query hashing

patterns.name: GraphQL Cursor-Based Pagination (Connections)
patterns.description: The Relay-style connection specification for paginating through lists in GraphQL. Uses cursors (opaque strings) rather than offsets, with edges containing nodes and cursors, and pageInfo providing hasNextPage/hasPreviousPage. This is the community-standard approach for GraphQL pagination.
principles: Cursors are opaque strings, not page numbers or offsets
Connection type wraps edges, each edge has a node and cursor
PageInfo includes hasNextPage, hasPreviousPage, startCursor, endCursor
Forward pagination uses first + after, backward uses last + before
Total count is optional and should be omitted if expensive to compute
when_to_use: Any GraphQL list field that may return many items
Infinite scroll UIs consuming GraphQL APIs
Relay-based frontend applications requiring connection-compliant schema
Datasets where items are inserted or deleted frequently
APIs needing stable pagination that does not skip or duplicate items
avoid_when: Simple lists that are always small and complete
Offset-based pagination is explicitly required by client design
Schema simplicity is prioritised over pagination correctness
signals: Schema types named *Connection and *Edge
Fields accept first, after, last, before arguments
PageInfo type with hasNextPage, hasPreviousPage, startCursor, endCursor
Edge type contains node and cursor fields
Relay compiler or relay-style pagination hooks in client code
Database queries use keyset pagination to back cursor resolution
key_concepts: Connection type: edges + pageInfo
Edge type: node + cursor
PageInfo: hasNextPage, hasPreviousPage, startCursor, endCursor
Forward pagination: first N after cursor
Backward pagination: last N before cursor
Cursor encoding: typically base64(type:id) but opaque to client

patterns.name: gRPC Unary RPC
patterns.description: The simplest gRPC pattern: client sends a single request message and receives a single response message, similar to a function call over the network. Built on HTTP/2 with Protocol Buffers for serialization. Provides strong typing, code generation, and efficient binary transport.
principles: One request, one response per RPC call
Protocol Buffers define message and service contracts
Generated client stubs provide type-safe RPC invocation
Deadlines and cancellation propagate across service boundaries
Metadata (headers/trailers) carry cross-cutting concerns
Status codes are gRPC-specific (OK, NOT_FOUND, INTERNAL, etc.)
when_to_use: Synchronous request-response communication between microservices
High-throughput internal service-to-service communication
Polyglot environments needing cross-language type-safe contracts
Latency-sensitive paths where binary serialization matters
Services with well-defined request/response message shapes
avoid_when: Clients are web browsers without a gRPC-Web proxy
Human readability of messages is important for debugging
API consumers need a simple REST/JSON interface
Network infrastructure does not support HTTP/2
signals: Proto files define service with rpc methods returning single messages
Generated _grpc.pb files in build output
Client code calls stub methods with request objects
gRPC interceptors in the middleware chain
Health check service implements grpc.health.v1.Health
Deadline/timeout propagation in call options
key_concepts: Protocol Buffers (protobuf) message and service definition
Code generation: protoc compiler with language-specific plugins
Channel and stub abstractions
Deadline propagation across service calls
gRPC status codes and error model
Interceptors for logging, auth, and metrics

patterns.name: gRPC Server Streaming RPC
patterns.description: Client sends a single request and the server returns a stream of response messages. The client reads from the stream until there are no more messages. Useful for large result sets, real-time feeds, and download-style operations where the server pushes data progressively.
principles: Server sends multiple messages in response to a single client request
Client reads messages sequentially from the response stream
Stream ends when server sends a trailing status
Flow control is managed by HTTP/2 at the transport level
Server can send status and trailers to signal completion or error
when_to_use: Streaming large datasets that would be inefficient as a single response
Real-time event feeds (price tickers, log streams, status updates)
Progressive data loading (e.g., search results as they are found)
File or data download with progress tracking
Subscribe-once patterns where client receives continuous updates
avoid_when: Response is small enough for a unary RPC
Client needs to send data during the stream (use bidirectional instead)
Load balancers cannot handle long-lived HTTP/2 streams
signals: Proto file defines rpc Method(Request) returns (stream Response)
Server code uses response_observer.on_next() or stream.send_message()
Client iterates over response stream in a loop
Connection duration is significantly longer than typical request-response
gRPC keepalive settings configured for long-lived streams
Retry logic handles stream interruption and reconnection
key_concepts: Server streaming RPC: single request, multiple responses
Stream lifecycle: open, data flow, half-close, completion
HTTP/2 flow control for backpressure
Keepalive pings to maintain long-lived streams
Stream cancellation from client side
Error handling: status codes on stream termination

patterns.name: gRPC Client Streaming RPC
patterns.description: Client sends a stream of messages to the server and receives a single response after the server has processed all client messages. Used for upload-style operations, aggregation, and collecting a sequence of inputs before producing a result.
principles: Client sends multiple messages, server returns one response
Server waits for all client messages before responding (or responds early)
Client signals completion by half-closing the stream
Flow control ensures the server is not overwhelmed by fast clients
Server can send an early error status before reading all messages
when_to_use: File upload or chunked data ingestion
Aggregating sensor data or time-series readings for batch processing
Collecting a set of inputs before running a computation
Client sends a variable-length sequence of items for server-side storage
Telemetry collection from client agents
avoid_when: Client has a fixed single message to send (use unary)
Server needs to respond to each message individually (use bidirectional)
Stream reliability requirements exceed what gRPC retry provides
signals: Proto file defines rpc Method(stream Request) returns (Response)
Client code calls stream.send() in a loop then stream.close()
Server accumulates messages before returning a single response
Upload progress tracked by counting sent messages
Retry logic must handle partial stream resend on failure
Buffering configuration set for client-side message batching
key_concepts: Client streaming RPC: multiple requests, single response
Half-close: client signals no more messages
Server-side accumulation and aggregation
Client-side flow control and buffering
Chunked upload patterns
Early termination: server responds before reading all messages

patterns.name: gRPC Bidirectional Streaming RPC
patterns.description: Both client and server send streams of messages independently over a single RPC call. Messages can be sent in any order; the two streams operate independently. This is the most flexible gRPC pattern, suitable for chat, collaborative editing, and interactive protocols.
principles: Both client and server send message streams independently
Message ordering is preserved within each stream direction
Either side can finish sending without closing the connection
Full-duplex communication over a single HTTP/2 connection
Complex interaction protocols can be modelled as message sequences
when_to_use: Chat or messaging systems requiring full-duplex communication
Interactive protocols where client and server exchange messages freely
Real-time collaboration (cursor tracking, shared editing)
Game server communication with simultaneous input and state updates
Negotiation protocols requiring multiple rounds of exchange
avoid_when: Interaction is strictly request-response (use unary)
Only one direction needs streaming (use server or client streaming)
Infrastructure does not support long-lived HTTP/2 connections
Operational complexity of bidirectional streams is not justified
signals: Proto file defines rpc Method(stream Request) returns (stream Response)
Both client and server have send/receive loops running concurrently
Connection stays open for extended periods with messages in both directions
Application-level protocol defines message sequencing and acknowledgement
Keepalive and reconnection logic more complex than unary RPCs
Load balancing requires stream-aware (L7) infrastructure
key_concepts: Bidirectional streaming: both sides send message streams
Independent streams: send and receive operate separately
Message ordering guarantees per direction
Concurrent send/receive loops
Stream-aware load balancing requirements
Connection lifecycle management and reconnection

patterns.name: gRPC Service Definition and Interface Design
patterns.description: Best practices for designing gRPC service interfaces in Protocol Buffer files: naming conventions, method design, error handling, and API evolution. A well-designed service definition is the cornerstone of a maintainable gRPC ecosystem, serving as both documentation and code generation source.
principles: Service names use PascalCase, method names use PascalCase verbs
Request and response messages are named after the method (GetUserRequest/GetUserResponse)
Every RPC has its own request/response types (no reuse across methods)
Use wrapper types for optional fields in proto3
Error details use google.rpc.Status with typed detail messages
API design guides (e.g., Google AIP) provide consistent patterns
when_to_use: Starting a new gRPC service from scratch
Reviewing or refactoring existing proto definitions
Establishing organisational API design standards for gRPC
Training teams on Protocol Buffer best practices
Designing APIs that will be consumed by generated clients in multiple languages
avoid_when: Prototyping with JSON/REST is faster for initial exploration
Service interface is trivial and design rigour is overkill
Team is not familiar with Protocol Buffers
signals: Proto files follow consistent naming conventions
Dedicated request/response message per RPC method
Proto linting tools (buf, protolint) in CI pipeline
API design guide referenced in contribution documentation
Field numbers managed carefully to preserve backward compatibility
Google AIP patterns referenced in proto comments
key_concepts: Naming: PascalCase services, PascalCase methods, snake_case fields
Per-method request/response types
google.rpc.Status and error details for rich error responses
google.protobuf wrappers for nullable fields
Method options and custom annotations
API design guides (Google AIP, Buf style guide)

patterns.name: Protocol Buffer Schema Evolution
patterns.description: Rules and strategies for evolving Protocol Buffer schemas without breaking existing clients or servers. Covers field number management, backward/forward compatibility, and migration from proto2 to proto3. Proper schema evolution is critical for independently deployable services.
principles: Never reuse or change the type of a field number
Removing a field should reserve its number to prevent accidental reuse
Adding fields is always backward-compatible in proto3
Renaming fields does not break wire compatibility (numbers matter, not names)
Use oneof for mutually exclusive fields, not for evolution
Default values for missing fields must be safe for all consumers
when_to_use: Adding new fields to existing messages
Deprecating and removing fields from proto definitions
Migrating between proto2 and proto3 syntax
Ensuring independent deployment of producer and consumer services
Managing proto schemas across a large organisation
avoid_when: Schema changes require atomic deployment of all services (use a shared binary)
Wire format is not Protocol Buffers (JSON evolution rules differ)
Proto files are used only for code generation with no wire compatibility needs
signals: Proto files contain reserved field numbers or names
Buf breaking change detection in CI pipeline
Field numbers are monotonically increasing with no gaps from removal
Migration guides accompany major schema changes
Proto file version history tracked in source control
Compatibility testing between old and new schema versions
key_concepts: Field numbers: never reuse, reserve deprecated numbers
Wire compatibility: numbers and types matter, names do not
Reserved keyword: prevents accidental reuse of numbers/names
Backward compatibility: old readers can read new messages
Forward compatibility: new readers can read old messages
Buf CLI for schema linting and breaking change detection

patterns.name: WebSocket Communication Pattern
patterns.description: Full-duplex, persistent connection protocol over a single TCP connection, enabling real-time bidirectional communication between client and server. Initiated via HTTP upgrade handshake. Ideal for low-latency interactive applications where both client and server need to push messages independently.
principles: Full-duplex: both sides send messages independently at any time
Persistent connection reduces per-message overhead versus HTTP polling
Binary and text frame types support different payload formats
Ping/pong frames provide built-in keepalive mechanism
Connection state must be managed explicitly (open, closing, closed)
Horizontal scaling requires sticky sessions or external pub/sub
when_to_use: Real-time interactive applications (chat, gaming, collaborative editing)
Live data feeds requiring sub-second latency (trading, sports)
Applications needing bidirectional communication (not just server push)
Replacing long-polling where connection overhead is unacceptable
IoT device communication requiring persistent connections
avoid_when: Server-to-client push only is needed (SSE is simpler)
Client is behind restrictive proxies that block WebSocket upgrades
Stateless request-response semantics suffice
Scale requires millions of connections without infrastructure investment
signals: HTTP 101 Switching Protocols response in network traffic
ws:// or wss:// URLs in client configuration
WebSocket server library (ws, socket.io, websockets) in dependencies
Connection upgrade headers in initial HTTP handshake
Heartbeat/ping-pong implementation in application code
Connection manager tracking active WebSocket sessions
key_concepts: HTTP upgrade handshake (Connection: Upgrade, Upgrade: websocket)
Frame types: text, binary, ping, pong, close
Sub-protocols for application-level message contracts
Connection lifecycle: open, message, error, close events
Scaling: sticky sessions, Redis pub/sub for multi-instance
Reconnection strategies with exponential backoff

patterns.name: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
patterns.description: Unidirectional server-to-client streaming over standard HTTP, using the text/event-stream content type. Simpler than WebSockets for server push scenarios, with built-in browser support via EventSource API. Supports automatic reconnection and event ID tracking for resumption.
principles: Server pushes events to client over a long-lived HTTP response
Text-based protocol with event type, data, and ID fields
Built-in reconnection with Last-Event-ID header for resumption
Works through HTTP proxies and load balancers without special configuration
Unidirectional: client cannot send data over the same connection
when_to_use: Live notifications, alerts, or status updates to web clients
Dashboard widgets showing real-time metrics
Progress updates for long-running server operations
News or social media feed updates
Any server-push scenario where client-to-server messaging is not needed
avoid_when: Bidirectional communication is required
Binary data streaming is needed (SSE is text-only)
Thousands of concurrent connections per client are needed
Client must send frequent messages to the server
signals: Response Content-Type is text/event-stream
Client uses EventSource API or equivalent library
Event data lines prefixed with data:, event:, id:, retry:
Last-Event-ID header sent on reconnection
Server keeps response connection open with periodic events
CORS headers configured for cross-origin event streams
key_concepts: EventSource browser API with onmessage, onerror, onopen
Event stream format: data, event, id, retry fields
Automatic reconnection with Last-Event-ID resumption
Named events for routing to different handlers
Connection timeout and keepalive comment lines
Polyfill libraries for environments without native EventSource

patterns.name: Webhook Event Delivery
patterns.description: Server-initiated HTTP callbacks to a client-specified URL when events occur. The event producer POSTs event payloads to registered subscriber endpoints. Webhooks invert the polling relationship, enabling event-driven integration without persistent connections.
principles: Producer sends HTTP POST to subscriber-registered callback URLs
Payload includes event type, timestamp, and relevant data
Subscribers must respond with 2xx status to acknowledge receipt
Retry with exponential backoff on delivery failure
Webhook signatures (HMAC) verify payload authenticity
Idempotency: subscribers must handle duplicate deliveries gracefully
when_to_use: Integrating with third-party services that need to react to your events
Payment processing notifications (charge succeeded, refund issued)
CI/CD pipeline triggers from source control events
SaaS platforms notifying customer systems of data changes
Event-driven architectures across organisational boundaries
avoid_when: Consumer is not reachable via public URL (use polling or message queue)
Low-latency delivery is critical (network variability adds delay)
Event volume is very high (message queue is more efficient)
Delivery ordering guarantees are required
signals: API documentation describes webhook registration endpoints
Subscriber stores callback URL and secret in configuration
X-Webhook-Signature or similar HMAC header in event requests
Retry queue and dead letter storage for failed deliveries
Event payload includes event_type and idempotency_key fields
Webhook management UI for subscribers to register/test endpoints
key_concepts: Callback URL registration and management
HMAC signature verification for payload authenticity
Retry strategy with exponential backoff and jitter
Dead letter queue for permanently failed deliveries
Event payload envelope: type, timestamp, data, idempotency key
Webhook testing tools and event replay

patterns.name: Long Polling Pattern
patterns.description: Client sends an HTTP request that the server holds open until new data is available or a timeout expires, then responds. The client immediately sends another request, creating a near-real-time push effect over standard HTTP. A pragmatic fallback when WebSockets or SSE are unavailable.
principles: Server delays response until data is available or timeout is reached
Client immediately reconnects after receiving a response
Timeout prevents indefinitely held connections
Each response can include multiple accumulated events
Standard HTTP semantics: works through all proxies and firewalls
when_to_use: Near-real-time updates when WebSocket/SSE are not supported
Browser environments behind restrictive corporate proxies
Fallback transport for WebSocket libraries (e.g., Socket.IO)
Simple notification systems without infrastructure for push protocols
Legacy system integration where only HTTP is available
avoid_when: WebSockets or SSE are available and supported
Very high frequency updates make reconnection overhead prohibitive
Bidirectional communication is needed
Server resource consumption from held connections is a concern
signals: Client makes repeated HTTP requests in a loop with minimal delay
Server response times are highly variable (some instant, some timeout)
Request includes a version token or timestamp for change detection
Connection timeout configured between 20-60 seconds
Server holds request handlers in a pending queue awaiting data
key_concepts: Hold-until-data-or-timeout response pattern
Immediate reconnection after response
ETag or version token for change detection
Connection timeout tuning (balance between latency and resource usage)
Comet pattern (historical term for long polling)
Comparison with short polling, SSE, and WebSocket

patterns.name: Publish-Subscribe Messaging
patterns.description: Decoupled messaging pattern where publishers send messages to topics without knowledge of subscribers, and subscribers receive messages from topics they have registered interest in. The message broker handles routing, buffering, and delivery. Foundation of event-driven architectures.
principles: Publishers and subscribers are decoupled through topics/channels
Messages are delivered to all subscribers of a topic (fan-out)
Broker handles buffering, routing, and delivery guarantees
Topic-based or content-based filtering determines message routing
Subscription can be durable (survives disconnection) or ephemeral
Message ordering guarantees vary by broker and configuration
when_to_use: Multiple services need to react to the same event independently
Event-driven microservice architectures with loose coupling
Broadcasting state changes across system components
Notification systems with multiple delivery channels
Integration patterns where new consumers can be added without producer changes
avoid_when: Point-to-point communication between two known services
Strong ordering or exactly-once delivery is required without broker support
Request-response semantics are needed
Message volume does not justify broker infrastructure overhead
signals: Message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SNS, Pub/Sub) in infrastructure
Services publish events without referencing consumer names
Topic/channel names in configuration or code (e.g., orders.created)
Multiple services subscribe to the same event topic
Subscription management (subscribe, unsubscribe) in service startup
Fan-out delivery pattern visible in message flow diagrams
key_concepts: Topic: named channel for message routing
Publisher: sends messages to a topic without knowing subscribers
Subscriber: registers interest in a topic and receives messages
Fan-out: one message delivered to all subscribers
Durable subscriptions: messages buffered during subscriber downtime
Message filtering: topic-based, attribute-based, or content-based

patterns.name: Competing Consumers Pattern
patterns.description: Multiple consumer instances read from the same message queue, with each message delivered to exactly one consumer. This enables horizontal scaling of message processing by adding more consumer instances. The broker ensures each message is processed once through acknowledgement protocols.
principles: Each message is delivered to exactly one consumer in the group
Adding consumers increases processing throughput linearly
Message acknowledgement prevents loss on consumer failure
Failed messages are retried or moved to a dead letter queue
Consumer group coordination handled by the broker
Message ordering may be sacrificed for parallelism
when_to_use: Processing workload exceeds a single consumer's capacity
Work items are independent and can be processed in any order
Horizontal scaling of background job processing
Rate-limited API calls distributed across worker pool
CPU-intensive message processing that benefits from parallelism
avoid_when: Strict message ordering is required across all messages
Every consumer needs every message (use pub/sub fan-out instead)
Processing is already fast enough with a single consumer
Message interdependencies require coordinated processing
signals: Consumer group configuration in message broker settings
Multiple instances of the same service reading from one queue
Message visibility timeout or acknowledgement configuration
Auto-scaling rules based on queue depth
Dead letter queue for messages exceeding retry limits
Partition assignment in Kafka consumer groups
key_concepts: Consumer group: set of consumers sharing work from a queue
Message acknowledgement (ack/nack) for delivery guarantees
Visibility timeout: message hidden from other consumers during processing
Rebalancing: partition reassignment when consumers join/leave
At-least-once delivery: message redelivered on ack timeout
Idempotent processing: handle duplicate deliveries safely

patterns.name: Dead Letter Queue
patterns.description: A secondary queue that receives messages which cannot be successfully processed after a configured number of retry attempts. Prevents poison messages from blocking the main queue while preserving them for investigation, manual replay, or automated remediation.
principles: Messages move to DLQ after exceeding maximum retry count
Original message metadata (headers, timestamps, error details) is preserved
DLQ messages are inspectable for debugging and root cause analysis
Replay mechanisms allow reprocessing after fixing the underlying issue
DLQ depth is a key operational metric and alert trigger
DLQ messages should have a retention policy to prevent unbounded growth
when_to_use: Message processing can fail due to transient or permanent errors
Poison messages must not block processing of subsequent messages
Failed message investigation and replay is an operational requirement
Compliance requires preserving evidence of processing failures
Background job systems need visibility into permanently failed work items
avoid_when: Message loss is acceptable and monitoring retries is sufficient
All messages must be processed in strict order (DLQ breaks ordering)
System has no operational tooling to inspect or replay DLQ messages
signals: Queue configuration includes maxReceiveCount or max_delivery_attempts
Secondary queue named *-dlq or *-dead-letter in infrastructure
Monitoring dashboards show DLQ depth as a key metric
Operational runbooks describe DLQ investigation and replay procedures
Message broker configuration links main queue to dead letter queue
Error details attached as message attributes before DLQ routing
key_concepts: Maximum retry count: threshold before DLQ routing
Message metadata preservation (original headers, error traces)
DLQ inspection tooling for root cause analysis
Message replay: reprocessing DLQ messages after fixing issues
DLQ depth monitoring and alerting
Retention policy to bound DLQ storage

patterns.name: Message Ordering Guarantees
patterns.description: Strategies for maintaining message order in asynchronous messaging systems. Full ordering, partition-level ordering, and causal ordering offer different trade-offs between correctness and throughput. Most systems achieve partition-key ordering, where messages sharing a key are processed in order.
principles: Total ordering across all messages is expensive and rarely needed
Partition-key ordering (all messages with same key go to same partition) is the pragmatic default
Causal ordering ensures dependent messages are processed in sequence
Ordering and parallelism are inherently in tension
Consumer group rebalancing can temporarily disrupt ordering
Sequence numbers enable gap detection and reordering at the consumer
when_to_use: State machine transitions that must be applied in sequence
Event sourcing where aggregate events must be replayed in order
Financial transactions for the same account or instrument
User activity streams where action order matters for correctness
Change data capture events for the same database row
avoid_when: Messages are independent and processing order does not affect outcome
Throughput is prioritised over ordering correctness
Consumer-side reordering buffers add unacceptable latency
signals: Partition key or message key set to entity ID (user_id, order_id)
Single-partition topics used for strict ordering (limiting throughput)
Sequence number field in message payload
Consumer processes messages sequentially without parallel dispatch
Rebalancing events trigger flush-before-reassign logic
Ordering tests verify message sequence in integration suites
key_concepts: Partition-key ordering: same key -> same partition -> same consumer
Total ordering: single partition, single consumer (low throughput)
Causal ordering: happened-before relationships respected
Sequence numbers for gap detection
Consumer-side reordering buffer
Ordering vs parallelism trade-off

patterns.name: Fan-Out Messaging Pattern
patterns.description: A single message or event is duplicated and delivered to multiple downstream consumers or queues simultaneously. Used to trigger parallel processing pipelines, broadcast notifications, or replicate data to multiple systems. The broker or routing layer handles the duplication.
principles: One input message produces multiple output copies
Each downstream consumer receives an independent copy
Fan-out can be topic-based (pub/sub) or explicit (exchange routing)
Downstream consumers process independently and may fail independently
Fan-out increases total message volume multiplicatively
Consider eventual consistency across fan-out targets
when_to_use: One event triggers processing in multiple independent systems
Broadcasting notifications to multiple channels (email, SMS, push)
Replicating data to multiple data stores or caches
Feeding the same event to analytics, audit, and operational pipelines
Triggering parallel workflows from a single trigger event
avoid_when: Only one consumer needs each message (use point-to-point queue)
Fan-out volume overwhelms downstream systems
Transactional consistency across all fan-out targets is required
signals: SNS topic publishing to multiple SQS queues (cloud-specific: AWS fan-out)
RabbitMQ fanout exchange type in broker configuration
Kafka topic consumed by multiple independent consumer groups
Event router component with multiple downstream destinations
Same event type appears in logs of multiple independent services
Infrastructure diagram shows one-to-many message flow arrows
key_concepts: Fan-out exchange: broker duplicates messages to all bound queues
Topic-based fan-out: all subscribers to a topic receive the message
SNS-to-SQS fan-out (AWS pattern)
Independent failure handling per downstream consumer
Fan-out factor: number of copies per input message
Back-pressure propagation across fan-out targets

patterns.name: Relational Database Selection
patterns.description: Guidelines for choosing relational (SQL) databases as the primary data store. RDBMS provides ACID transactions, normalised schema, referential integrity, and a mature query language. Suitable for structured data with well-defined relationships and consistency requirements.
principles: ACID transactions guarantee consistency for multi-statement operations
Normalised schemas reduce data redundancy and update anomalies
Foreign keys enforce referential integrity at the storage level
SQL is a widely understood, declarative query language
Indexing strategies are critical for query performance at scale
Vertical scaling is natural; horizontal scaling requires sharding or read replicas
when_to_use: Transactional workloads requiring strong consistency (finance, inventory)
Data with well-defined schemas and stable relationships
Complex queries involving joins, aggregations, and subqueries
Regulatory environments requiring ACID compliance
Applications where the schema is known at design time
avoid_when: Schema is highly variable or unknown at design time
Write throughput exceeds vertical scaling limits
Data is naturally hierarchical or document-shaped
Graph traversal queries dominate the workload
Eventual consistency is acceptable and horizontal scale is paramount
signals: Domain model has entities with clear foreign key relationships
Transactions span multiple tables (e.g., debit account, credit account)
Reporting queries require multi-table joins and aggregations
Schema migration tools (Flyway, Alembic, Liquibase) in the project
Data access layer uses an ORM (SQLAlchemy, Hibernate, Prisma)
Compliance requirements reference ACID or transaction isolation levels
key_concepts: ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
Normalisation: 1NF through BCNF for schema design
Indexing: B-tree, hash, GIN, GiST for query optimisation
Transaction isolation levels: read committed, repeatable read, serializable
Connection pooling for concurrent access management
Read replicas for scaling read-heavy workloads

patterns.name: Document Database Selection
patterns.description: Guidelines for choosing document databases (e.g., MongoDB, CouchDB) where data is stored as self-contained JSON/BSON documents. Documents can have nested structures and variable schemas, making them suitable for content management, catalogs, and applications with evolving data models.
principles: Documents are self-contained units of data with nested structures
Schema flexibility allows different documents to have different shapes
Queries operate on document fields, including nested paths
Denormalisation is expected; embed related data for read performance
Horizontal scaling via sharding is a first-class capability
Transactions across documents are possible but more expensive than in RDBMS
when_to_use: Content management systems with variable content types
Product catalogs where items have different attribute sets
User profiles with varying fields and preferences
Rapid prototyping where schema is evolving frequently
Read-heavy workloads where denormalised documents reduce joins
avoid_when: Data requires multi-document transactions frequently
Strong referential integrity across entities is critical
Complex analytical queries with multi-collection joins
Write patterns update the same document from many concurrent writers
Regulatory requirements mandate strict schema enforcement
signals: Data naturally maps to JSON documents with nested objects and arrays
Different records of the same type have different fields
Read patterns fetch entire documents rather than individual fields
Application code works with document/object structures natively
Sharding key design discussions in architecture documents
MongoDB, CouchDB, or DynamoDB (document mode) in tech stack
key_concepts: Document: self-contained JSON/BSON data unit
Collection: group of documents (analogous to table)
Embedded documents vs references (embed vs link trade-off)
Flexible schema: schema validation optional, not enforced by default
Sharding: distribute documents across nodes by shard key
Change streams for real-time document change notifications

patterns.name: Graph Database Selection
patterns.description: Guidelines for choosing graph databases where data is modelled as nodes and edges with properties. Graph databases excel at relationship-heavy queries like social networks, fraud detection, and knowledge graphs. They provide index-free adjacency for traversal performance independent of total dataset size.
principles: Data is modelled as nodes (entities) and edges (relationships) with properties
Index-free adjacency enables constant-time relationship traversal
Graph queries express path patterns, not join operations
Schema-on-read allows flexible relationship modelling
Traversal depth is bounded by query, not by schema design
Graph visualisation aids understanding of complex relationship structures
when_to_use: Social network analysis (friends, followers, influence paths)
Fraud detection requiring multi-hop relationship analysis
Knowledge graphs and recommendation engines
Network topology and dependency mapping
Access control with complex hierarchical or role-based structures
avoid_when: Data is primarily tabular with few relationships
Aggregate queries (sum, count, average) dominate the workload
Write throughput is extremely high and graph integrity overhead is costly
Simple key-value lookups are the primary access pattern
signals: Domain model diagrams show dense interconnections between entities
Queries ask about paths, shortest routes, or transitive relationships
Requirements mention 'degrees of separation' or 'connected to'
Cypher, Gremlin, or SPARQL queries in the codebase
Graph visualisation tools used for data exploration
Relationship types are first-class domain concepts, not join tables
key_concepts: Node: entity with labels and properties
Edge: typed, directed relationship with properties
Cypher query language (Neo4j, Kuzu)
Index-free adjacency for O(1) traversal per hop
Property graph model vs RDF triple model
Graph algorithms: shortest path, PageRank, community detection

patterns.name: Key-Value Store Selection
patterns.description: Guidelines for choosing key-value stores (e.g., Redis, DynamoDB, etcd) where data is accessed by a unique key with no inherent structure imposed on the value. Offers the simplest data model with the highest read/write throughput and lowest latency. Suitable for caching, session storage, and configuration.
principles: Data is accessed exclusively by primary key (no secondary indexes by default)
Values are opaque blobs from the store's perspective
Reads and writes are O(1) by design
Horizontal scaling via consistent hashing or partitioning
TTL (time-to-live) enables automatic expiration for cache use cases
Simplest possible data model minimises operational complexity
when_to_use: Caching layers requiring sub-millisecond reads
Session storage for web applications
Feature flags and configuration management
Rate limiting counters and leaderboards
Distributed locking and coordination
avoid_when: Queries need to filter or search by non-key attributes
Data has complex relationships requiring joins
Transactions across multiple keys are frequently needed
Full-text search capabilities are required
signals: Access patterns are exclusively get-by-key and put-by-key
Sub-millisecond latency requirements in SLA documents
Cache-aside or cache-through patterns in application architecture
TTL configuration on stored values
Redis, Memcached, or DynamoDB in infrastructure
Session store configuration in web framework settings
key_concepts: Key design: hierarchical keys (user:123:session) for namespace organisation
TTL: automatic expiration for cache and ephemeral data
Consistent hashing for distributed key-value partitioning
Cache-aside pattern: check cache, miss falls through to primary store
Atomic operations: increment, compare-and-swap
Eviction policies: LRU, LFU, random for memory-bounded stores

patterns.name: Time-Series Database Selection
patterns.description: Guidelines for choosing time-series databases (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Prometheus) optimised for storing and querying timestamped data points. These databases provide specialised storage engines, compression, and query capabilities for metrics, IoT telemetry, and financial tick data.
principles: Data is organised by time, with timestamp as the primary dimension
Write-optimised: high-throughput append-only ingestion
Time-based queries (last hour, last 7 days) are first-class operations
Downsampling and retention policies manage storage growth
Specialised compression exploits temporal patterns in data
Tag-based indexing enables efficient filtering of series
when_to_use: Infrastructure monitoring and metrics collection
IoT sensor data ingestion and analysis
Financial market data (tick data, OHLCV)
Application performance monitoring (APM) data
Energy consumption or environmental monitoring
avoid_when: Data is not primarily time-indexed
Complex relational queries across non-temporal dimensions are needed
Update and delete operations on historical data are frequent
Dataset is small enough for a general-purpose database
signals: Data arrives as continuous streams of timestamped measurements
Queries filter primarily by time range and secondarily by tags
Dashboards show time-series charts (Grafana, Kibana)
Data retention policies define rollup and expiration rules
Write rates measured in millions of points per second
InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, or Prometheus in tech stack
key_concepts: Measurement/metric: named time series (cpu_usage, temperature)
Tags: indexed metadata for filtering (host, region, sensor_id)
Fields: non-indexed values (the actual measurement data)
Retention policies: auto-delete data older than threshold
Downsampling: aggregate high-resolution data into coarser intervals
Continuous queries/materialized views for pre-computed aggregations

patterns.name: Wide-Column Store Selection
patterns.description: Guidelines for choosing wide-column stores (e.g., Apache Cassandra, HBase, ScyllaDB) that organise data into rows and column families, allowing sparse and wide rows. Designed for extreme write throughput, horizontal scalability, and multi-datacenter replication at massive scale.
principles: Data is partitioned by row key across a cluster of nodes
Column families group related columns with independent storage settings
Schema design is driven by query patterns, not normalisation
Tunable consistency: choose consistency level per operation
Linear horizontal scaling by adding nodes to the cluster
Write path is optimised: append-only log + memtable + SSTable
when_to_use: Write-heavy workloads at massive scale (millions of writes/second)
Time-series or event logging where data is append-mostly
Multi-datacenter replication with tunable consistency
Applications with well-known query patterns that guide table design
IoT data ingestion across globally distributed clusters
avoid_when: Ad-hoc analytical queries are common (no flexible joins or aggregations)
Strong consistency across all operations is required
Data model requires complex relationships and foreign keys
Dataset is small enough that a single-node database suffices
signals: Table design documents start with query patterns, not entity relationships
Partition key and clustering key design discussed extensively
Consistency level (ONE, QUORUM, ALL) specified per query
Multi-datacenter replication topology in infrastructure
Cassandra, HBase, or ScyllaDB in tech stack
Compaction strategy selection in table DDL
key_concepts: Partition key: determines data distribution across nodes
Clustering key: determines sort order within a partition
Column family: group of columns stored together
Tunable consistency: choose consistency vs availability per operation
SSTable compaction strategies (size-tiered, leveled)
Anti-patterns: large partitions, tombstone accumulation

patterns.name: Search Engine Database Selection
patterns.description: Guidelines for choosing search engine databases (e.g., Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Typesense, Meilisearch) optimised for full-text search, faceted navigation, and ranked relevance queries. These systems use inverted indexes and analysers to enable fast, flexible text search across large document collections.
principles: Inverted index enables fast full-text search across large corpora
Analysers (tokenisers + filters) control how text is indexed and searched
Relevance scoring (TF-IDF, BM25) ranks results by match quality
Faceted search provides aggregated counts for filter navigation
Near-real-time indexing: documents searchable within seconds of ingestion
Horizontal scaling via sharding and replication
when_to_use: Full-text search across large collections of text documents
E-commerce product search with faceted filtering
Log aggregation and analysis (ELK/OpenSearch stack)
Autocomplete and typeahead search suggestions
Geospatial search queries (nearby, within radius)
avoid_when: Primary data store with ACID transaction requirements
Simple key-value lookups without text search
Data requiring strong consistency guarantees
Small datasets where database LIKE queries suffice
signals: Search bar is a primary UI element in the application
Requirements mention relevance ranking, facets, or fuzzy matching
Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Solr, Typesense, or Meilisearch in tech stack
Index mapping definitions in configuration files
Query DSL with bool, match, term, range queries in code
Log aggregation pipeline feeding into search cluster
key_concepts: Inverted index: token -> document ID mapping for fast lookup
Analyser pipeline: character filter -> tokeniser -> token filter
BM25 scoring: term frequency, inverse document frequency, length normalisation
Sharding: distribute index across multiple nodes
Mappings: field type definitions (text, keyword, date, geo_point)
Aggregations: bucket, metric, and pipeline aggregations for analytics

patterns.name: Multi-Model Database
patterns.description: Databases that support multiple data models (document, graph, key-value, relational) within a single engine, reducing operational complexity of running separate databases. Examples include ArangoDB, Cosmos DB, and SurrealDB. Trade-off is that specialised workloads may perform better on purpose-built engines.
principles: Single database engine supports multiple data models and query patterns
Reduces operational overhead of managing multiple database systems
Unified query language or API covers different data model operations
Cross-model queries possible within a single transaction
Jack-of-all-trades trade-off: may not match specialised database performance
Licensing and vendor lock-in considerations vary significantly
when_to_use: Application needs both document and graph queries on the same data
Startup or small team wanting to minimise infrastructure complexity
Prototyping where the optimal data model is not yet determined
Cross-model transactions are required without distributed coordination
Polyglot persistence overhead is unjustifiable for the team size
avoid_when: Workload is dominated by a single data model that a specialised DB handles better
Scale requirements exceed multi-model engine capabilities
Team has expertise in and infrastructure for specialised databases
Vendor lock-in to a specific multi-model platform is unacceptable
signals: Application queries data as both documents and graphs
Infrastructure team prefers managing fewer database systems
ArangoDB, Cosmos DB, SurrealDB, or similar in tech evaluation
Query language supports graph traversal and document retrieval
Data model requirements span multiple paradigms simultaneously
Architecture decision records cite operational simplicity as a driver
key_concepts: Multi-model engine: single system, multiple data paradigms
Unified query language (AQL, SQL-like with graph extensions)
Cross-model indexing and query optimisation
Operational simplification vs specialised performance trade-off
Consistency model across different data model operations
Migration path to specialised databases if needed

patterns.name: Polyglot Persistence
patterns.description: Using different database technologies for different data storage needs within the same application or system, choosing the best tool for each job. For example, a relational DB for transactions, a document DB for product catalog, and a search engine for full-text queries. Requires managing consistency across stores.
principles: Each data store is chosen for its strengths against specific access patterns
No single database is optimal for all query patterns
Data consistency across stores requires explicit synchronisation strategies
Operational complexity scales with the number of different systems
Service boundaries often align with data store boundaries
Eventually consistent synchronisation between stores is common
when_to_use: Application has fundamentally different data access patterns
Search, caching, transactions, and analytics each need optimised storage
Microservice architecture where each service owns its data store
Performance requirements vary dramatically across different data types
Team has expertise to operate multiple database technologies
avoid_when: Team lacks operational capacity for multiple database systems
Data model is homogeneous and a single database suffices
Consistency requirements make cross-store synchronisation too complex
Application scale does not justify infrastructure complexity
signals: Infrastructure includes multiple database technologies
Different services or modules use different data stores
Synchronisation jobs or CDC pipelines move data between stores
Architecture diagrams show data flowing to multiple storage systems
Database selection criteria documents weigh trade-offs per use case
Operational runbooks cover multiple database administration procedures
key_concepts: Right tool for the right job: match DB to access pattern
Data synchronisation: CDC, dual-write, or event-driven propagation
Operational overhead: monitoring, backup, and scaling for each system
Consistency challenges: eventual consistency between heterogeneous stores
Service-per-database alignment in microservice architecture
Migration and data gravity considerations

patterns.name: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) Pipeline
patterns.description: Data integration pattern that extracts data from source systems, transforms it (cleaning, enriching, aggregating) in a staging area, and loads the transformed data into a target system (typically a data warehouse). Transformation happens before loading, ensuring only clean, structured data enters the target.
principles: Extract from heterogeneous sources without modifying them
Transform in a staging area before loading into the target
Load transformed data in bulk into the destination system
Idempotent pipeline runs enable safe re-execution
Schema-on-write: data must conform to target schema before loading
Pipeline orchestration manages dependencies and scheduling
when_to_use: Loading data into a data warehouse with strict schema requirements
Integrating data from multiple source systems into a unified model
Data quality enforcement before loading (validation, deduplication)
Batch reporting and business intelligence data preparation
Regulatory reporting requiring auditable data transformations
avoid_when: Real-time or near-real-time data processing is required
Schema-on-read flexibility is preferred (use ELT instead)
Transformation logic is simple enough to do in the target system
Data volume is too large for batch transformation in staging
signals: Pipeline code has distinct extract, transform, and load stages
Staging tables or files hold intermediate transformation results
Scheduled batch jobs (cron, Airflow, Prefect) orchestrate pipelines
Data validation and quality checks between stages
Target system has a fixed star or snowflake schema
Tools like Apache NiFi, Talend, or dbt in the data stack
key_concepts: Extract: pull data from sources (APIs, databases, files)
Transform: clean, enrich, aggregate, join in staging
Load: write transformed data to target in bulk
Staging area: temporary storage for in-progress transformations
Orchestration: DAG-based pipeline dependency management
Idempotency: re-runnable pipelines producing the same result

patterns.name: ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) Pipeline
patterns.description: Data integration pattern that extracts raw data from sources, loads it directly into the target system (data warehouse or lakehouse), and then transforms it using the target's compute engine. Modern cloud data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery) make this efficient by providing scalable compute for in-place transformation.
principles: Load raw data first, transform later using target system compute
Schema-on-read: raw data is preserved, transformations are views or tables
Target system provides the compute power for transformation
Raw data preservation enables reprocessing with new transformation logic
Separation of ingestion and transformation concerns
Transformation logic is version-controlled SQL or similar
when_to_use: Cloud data warehouses with elastic compute (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift)
Raw data preservation is required for auditability or reprocessing
Transformation logic evolves frequently and reprocessing is common
Data analysts own transformation logic in SQL using tools like dbt
Data volume is large and staging area storage is expensive
avoid_when: Target system lacks compute power for transformation at scale
Data must be cleaned before loading due to compliance requirements
Target system charges per query and transformation cost is a concern
On-premises systems with limited compute for post-load transformation
signals: Raw data tables or staging schemas in the data warehouse
dbt (data build tool) models defining transformations in SQL
Ingestion pipelines (Fivetran, Airbyte, Stitch) loading raw data
Transformation DAGs separate from ingestion pipeline configuration
Data warehouse costs dominated by transformation queries
Version-controlled SQL models in a dbt project structure
key_concepts: Extract: pull raw data from sources
Load: write raw data directly to target system
Transform: use target system compute (SQL) to shape data
dbt: SQL-first transformation framework for ELT
Raw layer -> staging layer -> mart layer data modelling
Reprocessing: re-run transformations on preserved raw data

patterns.name: Change Data Capture (CDC)
patterns.description: Capturing row-level changes (inserts, updates, deletes) from a database's transaction log and propagating them as events to downstream consumers. CDC enables near-real-time data synchronisation without polling or application-level dual-writes. Debezium is the most widely used open-source CDC platform.
principles: Changes are captured from the database transaction log, not by polling
Each change event includes before and after state of the row
CDC does not impact source database performance significantly
Events are ordered by transaction commit order
Schema changes in the source must be handled gracefully
Exactly-once semantics require coordination between CDC and consumer
when_to_use: Synchronising data between operational database and analytics systems
Building materialised views or search indexes from database changes
Event sourcing retrofit: derive events from an existing database
Cache invalidation triggered by database changes
Microservice data replication without application-level dual-write
avoid_when: Source database does not expose a transaction log (or access is restricted)
Application-level events already capture all relevant state changes
Data synchronisation latency of hours is acceptable (batch ETL is simpler)
Schema changes are so frequent that CDC event evolution is unmanageable
signals: Debezium connectors configured for source databases
Database binary log (MySQL binlog, PostgreSQL WAL) access enabled
Kafka topics named with database.schema.table naming convention
CDC event payloads include before/after snapshots of rows
Connector status monitoring in operational dashboards
