Schema registry storing CDC event schemas
key_concepts: Transaction log tailing: reading database WAL/binlog
Change event: operation type + before/after row state
Debezium: open-source CDC platform on Kafka Connect
Snapshot: initial full load before streaming incremental changes
Schema evolution: handling source schema changes in CDC events
Outbox pattern: reliable event publishing via CDC on an outbox table

patterns.name: Batch Processing Pattern
patterns.description: Processing large volumes of data in discrete, scheduled batches rather than continuously. Data is collected over a period, then processed as a complete set. Suitable for workloads where latency of minutes to hours is acceptable and throughput efficiency matters more than responsiveness.
principles: Process bounded datasets at scheduled intervals
Optimise for throughput rather than latency
Checkpoint and restart capabilities for fault tolerance
Partition data for parallel processing across workers
Input data is immutable during batch execution
Results are atomic: all-or-nothing for consistency
when_to_use: Nightly reports, billing calculations, or statement generation
Large-scale data transformations (ETL, model training)
Processing accumulated events or logs periodically
Data migration and backfill operations
Regulatory reporting with end-of-day deadlines
avoid_when: Sub-second or real-time response is required
Data must be processed as it arrives
Batch window is too short for the volume of data
Users expect immediate reflection of their actions
signals: Cron schedules or orchestrator DAGs trigger processing jobs
Processing operates on date-partitioned data sets
Job logs show start time, end time, and records processed
Spark, Hadoop MapReduce, or batch frameworks in the stack
SLA defined in terms of batch completion time, not latency
Data files land in staging directories before processing
key_concepts: Batch window: time period allocated for processing
Partitioning: divide data for parallel processing
Checkpointing: save progress for restart after failure
Idempotent batches: re-running produces same result
Batch scheduling: cron, Airflow, Step Functions
Batch vs micro-batch vs stream processing spectrum

patterns.name: Stream Processing Pattern
patterns.description: Processing data continuously as it arrives, record by record or in small windows, with low latency. Stream processing engines (Flink, Kafka Streams, Spark Structured Streaming) maintain state across events and produce results incrementally. Enables real-time analytics, alerting, and event-driven computation.
principles: Process each event as it arrives, not in batches
Maintain state across events for aggregation and windowing
Event time vs processing time distinction is critical for correctness
Exactly-once or at-least-once processing semantics must be configured
Backpressure mechanisms prevent consumer overwhelm
Late-arriving events require watermark and allowed lateness configuration
when_to_use: Real-time dashboards and monitoring with sub-second latency
Fraud detection requiring immediate analysis of transaction patterns
IoT sensor data processing with continuous alerting
Real-time recommendation or personalisation engines
Event-driven microservice coordination
avoid_when: Processing latency of minutes or hours is acceptable
Data arrives in infrequent large batches
Computation requires full dataset access (not incremental)
Team lacks experience with stream processing complexity
signals: Apache Flink, Kafka Streams, or Spark Structured Streaming in stack
Processing code references windows, watermarks, or event time
Metrics show end-to-end latency in milliseconds or seconds
Consumer lag monitoring on streaming topics
State stores (RocksDB, changelog topics) in processor configuration
Exactly-once or at-least-once guarantees discussed in design docs
key_concepts: Event time vs processing time
Windowing: tumbling, sliding, session windows
Watermarks: tracking event time progress for late data handling
State management: local state stores with changelog backup
Exactly-once semantics: transactional source-to-sink processing
Backpressure: flow control when consumers are slower than producers

patterns.name: Micro-Batch Processing
patterns.description: Processing data in very small, frequent batches (seconds to minutes) rather than true record-at-a-time streaming or large periodic batches. Provides a pragmatic middle ground with near-real-time latency, simpler exactly-once semantics than true streaming, and batch-like operational simplicity. Spark Structured Streaming uses this approach.
principles: Small time-bounded batches provide near-real-time results
Batch semantics simplify exactly-once processing guarantees
Trigger interval controls the latency-throughput trade-off
Checkpoint-based recovery reprocesses only the failed micro-batch
Compatible with batch processing frameworks and operators
Easier to reason about than continuous stream processing
when_to_use: Near-real-time processing where seconds of latency is acceptable
Teams experienced with batch processing adopting streaming
Exactly-once semantics needed without stream processing complexity
Spark-based data platform extending to near-real-time workloads
Transitioning from batch to streaming incrementally
avoid_when: True sub-second latency is required (use true streaming)
Event-at-a-time processing semantics are needed
Batch interval overhead is significant for small data volumes
Stateful stream processing with complex windowing is required
signals: Spark Structured Streaming with trigger interval configuration
Processing logs show batch-like execution every few seconds
Checkpoint directories with batch-numbered metadata
Latency measured in seconds rather than milliseconds
Batch-oriented monitoring (batch duration, records per batch)
Trigger.ProcessingTime or similar interval configuration
key_concepts: Trigger interval: time between micro-batch executions
Checkpoint: persistent state for exactly-once recovery
Micro-batch boundary: time-based or size-based
Latency-throughput trade-off via trigger interval tuning
Batch-compatible APIs for familiar programming model
Continuous processing mode as an alternative (Spark)

patterns.name: Data Lake Pattern
patterns.description: Centralised repository that stores raw data in its native format (structured, semi-structured, unstructured) at any scale, using schema-on-read rather than schema-on-write. Built on object storage (S3, GCS, ADLS) with a catalog layer for discoverability. Enables diverse analytics without upfront schema design.
principles: Store raw data in native format without upfront transformation
Schema-on-read: structure is applied at query time, not ingestion
Object storage provides virtually unlimited, cost-effective storage
Metadata catalog (Hive, Glue, Unity) enables data discoverability
Data governance prevents the lake from becoming a data swamp
Zone architecture: raw -> curated -> consumption layers
when_to_use: Collecting diverse data types for future, not-yet-known analytics
Machine learning pipelines requiring access to raw feature data
Centralising data from many heterogeneous source systems
Cost-effective storage of large volumes of rarely-queried data
Data science exploration requiring flexible access to raw data
avoid_when: All data is structured and schema-on-write suffices
Data governance maturity is insufficient (risk of data swamp)
Query latency requirements demand pre-structured data
Organisation lacks data engineering capacity to manage the lake
signals: Data stored in S3, GCS, or ADLS buckets with folder-based partitioning
Hive metastore, AWS Glue Catalog, or Unity Catalog for metadata
Multiple file formats: Parquet, ORC, JSON, CSV, Avro in the lake
Data zones: bronze/raw, silver/curated, gold/consumption
Spark, Presto, or Trino used for querying data in the lake
Data governance tools (Great Expectations, dbt tests) validating lake data
key_concepts: Object storage: S3, GCS, ADLS as the storage layer
Schema-on-read: apply schema at query time via external tables
Metadata catalog: centralised table/schema registry for discovery
Zone architecture: raw -> curated -> consumption data layers
File formats: Parquet (columnar), ORC, Avro (row-based)
Data swamp anti-pattern: ungoverned lake with no metadata

patterns.name: Data Warehouse Pattern
patterns.description: Centralised, schema-on-write analytical data store optimised for complex queries, aggregations, and reporting. Data is transformed and loaded into dimensional models (star/snowflake schemas) for fast analytical query performance. Cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) provide elastic compute separated from storage.
principles: Schema-on-write: data conforms to the target schema before loading
Dimensional modelling (star/snowflake) optimises analytical queries
Separation of storage and compute enables independent scaling
Columnar storage format accelerates aggregation and scan queries
Historical data preserved for trend analysis and time-series comparison
Access control and governance enforce data security policies
when_to_use: Business intelligence dashboards and ad-hoc analytical queries
Centralised reporting across multiple operational systems
Historical trend analysis and forecasting
Data-driven decision making requiring complex aggregations
Regulatory reporting with auditable, structured data
avoid_when: Real-time operational queries are the primary use case
Data is primarily unstructured (images, video, free text)
Write-heavy transactional workloads
Schema is unknown or changes too frequently for dimensional modelling
signals: Star or snowflake schema with fact and dimension tables
Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, or Synapse in tech stack
BI tools (Looker, Tableau, Power BI) connected to the warehouse
dbt models or ETL pipelines loading dimensional tables
Warehouse costs tracked by compute credits or query bytes scanned
Data governance policies and access control on warehouse schemas
key_concepts: Star schema: fact tables surrounded by dimension tables
Snowflake schema: normalised dimension tables
Slowly changing dimensions (SCD): Type 1, 2, 3 for historical tracking
Columnar storage: column-oriented format for analytical performance
Compute-storage separation: scale query compute independently
Materialised views for pre-computed query acceleration

patterns.name: Data Lakehouse Pattern
patterns.description: Architecture combining data lake flexibility (open formats, schema-on-read, raw data storage) with data warehouse reliability (ACID transactions, schema enforcement, governance). Implemented via table formats like Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, or Apache Hudi on top of object storage.
principles: ACID transactions on data lake storage (object store files)
Open table formats (Delta, Iceberg, Hudi) provide warehouse features
Schema evolution without full data rewrite
Time travel: query data as of a previous version or timestamp
Unified batch and streaming data processing on the same tables
Metadata layer enables partition pruning and data skipping
when_to_use: Unifying data lake and data warehouse into a single architecture
Requiring ACID transactions on data stored in object storage
Both BI/SQL analytics and ML workloads on the same data
Streaming and batch data landing in the same tables
Organisations wanting to avoid data duplication between lake and warehouse
avoid_when: Simple data lake without transaction or consistency needs
Existing data warehouse meets all requirements without lake flexibility
Team lacks experience with lakehouse table formats
Data volume is small enough that a traditional warehouse suffices
signals: Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, or Apache Hudi table format in use
Transaction log (delta_log, metadata) alongside data files in object storage
Time travel queries (VERSION AS OF, TIMESTAMP AS OF) in SQL
Unified Spark/Trino/Presto queries over lakehouse tables
Schema evolution operations (add column, rename) without data rewrite
Databricks, Tabular, or Onehouse in the platform stack
key_concepts: Table format: Delta Lake, Iceberg, Hudi metadata layer on files
ACID on object storage: transaction log ensures consistency
Time travel: query historical versions of data
Schema evolution: add, rename, reorder columns safely
Partition evolution: change partitioning without data rewrite (Iceberg)
Merge-on-read vs copy-on-write update strategies

patterns.name: Apache Kafka Streaming Platform
patterns.description: Distributed event streaming platform providing durable, ordered, partitioned logs of events. Kafka serves as the backbone for event-driven architectures, enabling publish-subscribe, stream processing, and event sourcing at scale. Its append-only log model provides replay capability and decouples producers from consumers.
principles: Append-only log: events are immutable once written
Partitioning enables parallel production and consumption
Consumer groups provide competing consumer semantics
Retention policy determines how long events are available for replay
Ordering guaranteed within a partition, not across partitions
Producers are decoupled from consumers through topic abstraction
when_to_use: Event-driven microservice architectures requiring durable messaging
High-throughput event ingestion (millions of events per second)
Event sourcing requiring an immutable, replayable event log
Stream processing pipelines (Kafka Streams, ksqlDB, Flink on Kafka)
Data integration backbone connecting multiple systems
avoid_when: Message volume is low and a simpler message broker suffices
Request-response communication patterns dominate
Operational complexity of Kafka cluster management is unjustifiable
Messages are transient and replay/durability is not needed
signals: Kafka brokers and ZooKeeper/KRaft in infrastructure
Topic names following domain.event.version naming convention
Producer and consumer client libraries in service dependencies
Consumer group IDs in application configuration
Kafka Connect connectors for data integration
Schema Registry (Confluent or Apicurio) managing event schemas
key_concepts: Topic: named category for organising events
Partition: ordered, immutable sequence of events within a topic
Consumer group: set of consumers sharing partition assignments
Offset: position of a consumer within a partition
Retention: time-based or size-based event retention policy
Compacted topic: keeps latest value per key (table semantics)

patterns.name: Event Log / Event Store Pattern
patterns.description: Persisting all state changes as an immutable, append-only sequence of events rather than storing only current state. The event log is the source of truth; current state is derived by replaying events. Enables audit trails, temporal queries, and rebuilding state from any point in history.
principles: Events are immutable facts: something that happened, not something to do
Current state is derived by replaying events from the log
Append-only: events are never updated or deleted
Event log provides complete audit trail by construction
Projections build read-optimised views from the event stream
Snapshots prevent unbounded replay time for long-lived aggregates
when_to_use: Audit trail requirements where every change must be recorded
Event sourcing architectures deriving state from events
Systems requiring temporal queries (what was the state at time T?)
Debugging production issues by replaying exact event sequences
Building multiple read models from the same event stream
avoid_when: Simple CRUD operations where current state is sufficient
Event schema evolution complexity is unacceptable
Team lacks experience with event sourcing patterns
Storage costs for unbounded event history are prohibitive
signals: Database table or stream storing events with sequence numbers
Event types named in past tense (OrderPlaced, PaymentReceived)
State reconstruction code that replays events sequentially
Snapshot mechanism for long-lived aggregates
EventStore, Kafka compacted topics, or custom event tables
Multiple read models (projections) built from the same events
key_concepts: Event: immutable fact with type, timestamp, and payload
Event stream: ordered sequence of events for an aggregate
Projection: read model derived from replaying events
Snapshot: cached state at a point to avoid full replay
Event versioning and upcasting for schema evolution
Idempotent event handlers for safe replay

patterns.name: Event Replay Pattern
patterns.description: Re-processing historical events from an event log or stream to rebuild state, backfill new projections, or recover from errors. Replay leverages the immutability of event logs to reconstruct any past state or build new derived views without affecting the live system.
principles: Events are replayed from a known starting position (offset, timestamp, or sequence number)
Replay consumers are isolated from live processing pipelines
Idempotent processing ensures replay produces correct results
Replay speed depends on event volume and processing complexity
New projections or read models can be built by replaying the full log
Replay must handle schema evolution across historical events
when_to_use: Building a new read model or projection from existing events
Recovering from a bug that corrupted derived state
Backfilling data after deploying a new feature
Testing new processing logic against historical event data
Migrating from one system to another using historical events
avoid_when: Event log is not retained long enough for the required replay window
Events contain side effects that should not be repeated (e.g., sending emails)
Replay processing time exceeds the available maintenance window
Events are not idempotent and deduplication is impractical
signals: Consumer offset reset to earliest or specific timestamp
Separate consumer group created for replay processing
Replay job configuration specifies start/end offsets or timestamps
Event handlers check idempotency keys before applying side effects
Documentation describes replay procedures for building new projections
Schema evolution handling (upcasters) in event processing code
key_concepts: Offset reset: rewind consumer to a specific position
Isolated replay consumer: separate from live processing
Idempotent processing: handle re-delivered events safely
Upcasting: transforming old event versions to current schema
Side-effect isolation: skip external calls during replay
Replay throughput: optimisations for fast historical reprocessing

patterns.name: Stream Join Pattern
patterns.description: Joining two or more event streams in real-time based on a common key within a time window. Stream joins combine events from different sources (e.g., clicks and impressions, orders and payments) to produce enriched output events. Windowed joins handle the temporal nature of streaming data.
principles: Join events from different streams by a shared key within a time window
Window size determines how long to wait for matching events
Inner, left, outer join semantics apply to stream joins
State stores hold buffered events waiting for join matches
Late arrivals beyond the window are either dropped or handled specially
Stream-table joins enrich stream events with static reference data
when_to_use: Correlating events from two different systems (clicks + conversions)
Enriching transaction streams with customer profile data
Matching order events with corresponding payment events
Combining sensor readings from co-located devices
Real-time fraud detection requiring cross-stream correlation
avoid_when: Events to join arrive days apart (window state becomes too large)
Simple lookup enrichment suffices (use stream-table join instead)
Join key cardinality is extremely high, causing state explosion
Batch processing can perform the join with acceptable latency
signals: Stream processing code uses join operations with window specifications
Two or more input topics joined by a common key field
Window duration configuration (e.g., 5 minutes, 1 hour) in join logic
State store size monitoring for join buffers
Unmatched event handling (left join, timeout callbacks)
Stream-table join using KTable, GlobalKTable, or similar abstractions
key_concepts: Windowed join: match events within a time window
Stream-stream join: both sides are unbounded event streams
Stream-table join: enrich stream with latest state from a table
Join key: field used to match events across streams
Window types for joins: sliding, tumbling, session
State management for buffered unmatched events

patterns.name: Windowed Aggregation Pattern
patterns.description: Grouping streaming events into time-based or count-based windows and computing aggregate functions (count, sum, average, min, max) over each window. Windows can be tumbling (fixed, non-overlapping), sliding (fixed, overlapping), or session-based (gap-driven). Core primitive for real-time analytics.
principles: Windows bound unbounded streams into finite, computable groups
Event time windowing provides deterministic results regardless of processing delay
Watermarks determine when a window is considered complete
Late events can trigger window re-computation or be discarded
Window state must be managed and expired to prevent unbounded growth
Aggregation results emitted on window close or incrementally
when_to_use: Real-time metrics: requests per second, average response time
Anomaly detection: unusual patterns within a time window
Session analysis: user activity grouped by session gaps
Rate limiting: counting events per key within a sliding window
IoT: sensor reading aggregations over fixed time intervals
avoid_when: Aggregation over the entire history is needed (not windowed)
Events have no meaningful temporal ordering
Batch aggregation meets latency requirements
Window state memory exceeds available resources
signals: Stream processing code defines tumbling, sliding, or session windows
Window duration and slide interval parameters in configuration
Watermark strategy configuration for late event handling
Aggregate functions (count, sum, avg) applied within window context
Window state store size monitoring and expiration settings
Allowed lateness configuration for late-arriving events
key_concepts: Tumbling window: fixed-size, non-overlapping time intervals
Sliding window: fixed-size, overlapping intervals with slide step
Session window: gap-driven, per-key grouping by inactivity timeout
Watermark: tracks progress of event time for window completion
Allowed lateness: grace period for late events after watermark
Window trigger: when to emit results (on close, early, late)

patterns.name: Exactly-Once Processing Semantics
patterns.description: Guaranteeing that each event in a stream is processed exactly once, producing the correct result even in the presence of failures, retries, and redelivery. Achieved through a combination of idempotent producers, transactional writes, and consumer offset management. The hardest delivery guarantee to implement correctly.
principles: Exactly-once is achieved end-to-end, not just at one layer
Idempotent producers prevent duplicate writes on retry
Transactional processing coordinates offset commits with output writes
Consumer-side deduplication handles at-least-once delivery from the broker
Exactly-once adds latency and throughput overhead
Simpler at-least-once with idempotent consumers is often sufficient
when_to_use: Financial transactions where double-processing has monetary impact
Aggregation counters that must be precisely accurate
Event sourcing systems where duplicate events corrupt state
Billing or metering systems where overcounting is unacceptable
Regulatory reporting requiring provably correct data processing
avoid_when: At-least-once with idempotent consumers is good enough
Throughput and latency requirements conflict with transactional overhead
System complexity budget cannot accommodate exactly-once mechanisms
Processing is naturally idempotent (e.g., overwriting a cache entry)
signals: Kafka transactional producer configuration (transactional.id, enable.idempotence)
Consumer-side deduplication using event ID or offset tracking
Read-process-write transactions spanning input and output topics
Isolation level read_committed configured on consumers
Flink checkpointing with exactly-once sink configuration
Idempotency key fields in event payloads
key_concepts: Idempotent producer: broker deduplicates retried writes
Transactional producer: atomic write of messages + offset commit
Consumer isolation level: read_committed skips uncommitted messages
End-to-end exactly-once: source -> processing -> sink coordination
Flink two-phase commit for exactly-once sinks
Trade-off: correctness vs throughput and complexity

patterns.name: Data Contract Pattern
patterns.description: Formal agreement between data producers and consumers specifying the schema, semantics, quality guarantees, and SLAs of a data product. Data contracts make data dependencies explicit, enabling independent evolution of producer and consumer while maintaining compatibility. Essential for data mesh and large-scale data platforms.
principles: Contract defines schema, semantics, quality rules, and SLAs explicitly
Producer owns the contract and is responsible for compliance
Breaking changes require contract versioning and consumer notification
Automated validation enforces contract compliance in CI/CD
Contracts are discoverable through a central catalog or registry
Quality metrics (freshness, completeness, accuracy) are part of the contract
when_to_use: Multiple teams consume data produced by a single team
Data quality issues have caused production incidents
Data mesh architecture requires explicit data product interfaces
Schema evolution must be managed without breaking consumers
Data governance requires documentation of data flows and ownership
avoid_when: Single team produces and consumes all data
Data schema is trivial and unlikely to change
Organisation lacks maturity for contract enforcement processes
Overhead of contract management exceeds the benefit
signals: YAML or JSON contract definitions in source control
Schema validation in CI/CD pipeline (contract tests)
Data catalog entries reference contract specifications
SLA dashboards tracking contract compliance metrics
Contract versioning following semantic versioning principles
Producer/consumer communication channels for contract changes
key_concepts: Schema specification: field names, types, nullability, constraints
Semantic specification: business meaning of fields and values
Quality guarantees: freshness, completeness, accuracy thresholds
SLA: availability, latency, and throughput commitments
Contract versioning: breaking vs non-breaking changes
Automated compliance testing in CI/CD

patterns.name: Schema Registry Pattern
patterns.description: Centralised service that stores, versions, and validates schemas for streaming data (Avro, Protobuf, JSON Schema). Producers register schemas before publishing, and consumers fetch schemas for deserialization. The registry enforces compatibility rules (backward, forward, full) to prevent breaking changes.
principles: Schemas are registered before data is produced
Compatibility checking prevents breaking schema changes
Schema ID embedded in message enables consumer-side deserialization
Backward compatibility: new schema can read old data
Forward compatibility: old schema can read new data
Subject naming strategy ties schemas to topics or record types
when_to_use: Kafka or other streaming platforms with schema evolution needs
Multiple producers and consumers sharing event schemas
Preventing schema-incompatible messages from entering topics
Automated schema validation in CI/CD pipelines
Centralised schema documentation and discovery
avoid_when: All producers and consumers are deployed atomically
Schema changes are extremely rare and manual coordination suffices
Message format is self-describing (JSON with no schema enforcement)
Operational overhead of running a schema registry is unjustifiable
signals: Confluent Schema Registry, Apicurio, or AWS Glue Schema Registry in stack
Avro, Protobuf, or JSON Schema definitions in source control
Schema ID bytes prepended to serialized messages
Compatibility mode (BACKWARD, FORWARD, FULL) configured per subject
CI pipeline validates schema compatibility before merge
Schema registry URL in producer and consumer configuration
key_concepts: Schema subject: named scope for schema versions (topic-value, record name)
Compatibility modes: BACKWARD, FORWARD, FULL, NONE
Schema ID: unique identifier embedded in serialized messages
Schema evolution: adding/removing fields within compatibility rules
Serializer/deserializer integration with messaging clients
Schema registry REST API for registration and retrieval

patterns.name: Data Mesh Architecture Pattern
patterns.description: Decentralised data architecture where domain teams own, produce, and serve their data as products. Shifts from centralised data teams to federated ownership with self-serve data infrastructure and federated computational governance. Data products are discoverable, addressable, and governed through interoperable standards.
principles: Domain-oriented data ownership: teams own their data products
Data as a product: data has discoverability, quality, SLAs, and documentation
Self-serve data platform: infrastructure enables teams to publish and consume data autonomously
Federated computational governance: automated policies replace centralised gatekeeping
Interoperability standards ensure cross-domain data consumption
Data product thinking: treat data consumers as customers
when_to_use: Large organisations where centralised data teams are bottlenecks
Multiple domains produce data consumed across organisational boundaries
Domain teams have engineering capacity to own their data products
Centralised data warehouse has become an unmaintainable monolith
Data quality suffers because producers have no skin in the game
avoid_when: Organisation is small enough for a centralised data team to work effectively
Domain teams lack data engineering skills or capacity
Data governance maturity is insufficient for federated model
Organisational structure does not align with domain-oriented ownership
Investment in self-serve data platform is not feasible
signals: Data products registered in a central data catalog with ownership metadata
Domain teams publish data with documented schemas and SLAs
Self-serve data infrastructure for provisioning pipelines and storage
Federated governance policies automated through platform tooling
Data product interfaces defined as contracts with versioning
Cross-domain data consumption through standardised protocols
key_concepts: Data product: a domain's curated, documented, governed data offering
Domain ownership: the team producing data owns it as a product
Self-serve data platform: infrastructure abstractions for data teams
Federated computational governance: policy as code, not gatekeeping
Data product catalog: discovery layer for available data products
Interoperability: standardised formats, protocols, and metadata
